coredata.dc:title: A coordination-driven fluorescent platform for selective detection of valine in living cells and food samples based on dopamine-functionalized nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots and its construction of molecular logic gate
coredata.dc:creator: Qian, Liangliang
Zhen, Zhengan
Tang, Shipeng
Zhou, Chaoqun
Ji, Man
Liu, Bin
Fang, Yuying
Ou, Shengju
Cheng, Rumei
coredata.dc:description: The design of a fluorescent system for tracking valine remains a challenge till date. This work demonstrated the first use of the metal complex of dopamine-functionalized nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs-DA) as coordination-driven fluorescent platform for selective detection of valine in human serum and real food samples. The fluorescence of the N-GQDs-DA was quenched after the addition of Cr3+, which acts as both a fluorescence quencher and a receptor for target molecule. The quenched fluorescence of the N-GQDs-DA/Cr3+ sensing system was linearly restored as the valine concentration increased from 1.33 × 10−4 M to 62.5 × 10−4 M. Different coordination states of Cr3+ opened or blocked a PET course, which constructed “turn-off or turn-on” fluorescence switching. The “INHIBIT” logic gate operation can be carried out using the Cr3+ and valine as inputs and the emission bands EM 315 nm and EM 440 nm as outputs, indicating that they have promising applications in intelligent dual analyte sensing systems. MALDI-TOF mass spectra elucidated the formation of a low-fluorescent species [C55H32Cl3Cr2N4O10]+ and a high-fluorescent species [C70H58Cr2N7O14]+ without and with valine, respectively. This methodology provides a new concept for the design and development of highly selective optical system for single amino acid.
coredata.dcterms:subject: N-GQDs
Dopamine
Valine
Cr3+
Cell imaging
Logic gate

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of valine in the real samples
Sensing behavior of N-GQDs-DA/Cr3+ to valine and molecular mechanism
Detection in tap water, milk, and honey

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image, (b) AFM image, (c) Raman spectrum, and (d) XRD spectrum of N-GQDs-DA.
UV–vis spectra of (a) DA, N-GQDs and N-GQDs-DA; (b) N-GQDs-DA, N-GQDs-DA/Cr3+ and CrCl3 in aqueous solutions.

(a) Fluorescence spectra of N-GQDs-DA with different concentrations of Cr3+ ranging from 0 to 23.8 × 10−4 M, the insert pictures of (i) and (ii) were linear relationship plot between relative fluorescence intensity and Cr3+ concentrations at EM 315 nm and EM 440 nm range from 1.66 × 10−4 M to 23.8 × 10−4 M, respectively; (b) Fluorescence spectra of N-GQDs-DA/Cr3+ sensing device with various concentration of valine ranging from 0 to 62.5 × 10−4 M, the insert pictures of (iii) and (iv) were linear relationship plot between relative fluorescence intensity and valine concentration at EM 315 nm and EM 440 nm with the concentration ranging from 6.62 to 62.5 × 10−4 M and from 1.33 to 16.4 × 10−4 M; respectively.
(a) MALDI-TOF mass spectrum of N-GQDs-DA; (b) MALDI-TOF mass spectrum of N-GQDs-DA/Cr3+; (c) MALDI-TOF mass spectrum of N-GQDs-DA/Cr3+ with valine; (d) Proposal of N-GQDs-DA molecular structure.

Confocal microscopy images of ARPE-19 cells incubated with N-GQDs-DA/Cr3+ solution, followed by addition of (i) 0 μL and (ii) 5.0 μL, and (iii) 10.0 μL valine for another 0.5 h at 37 °C. ([N-GQDs-DA] is 10 mg/L, [ Cr3+] is 1.6 mM, and [valine] is 2 mM in various cultures).
Illustration of the N-GQDs-DA sensing system for the detection of Cr3+ and valine.

head.ce:title: A coordination-driven fluorescent platform for selective detection of valine in living cells and food samples based on dopamine-functionalized nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots and its construction of molecular logic gate

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Advanced Materials for Nano-Bio Applications, School of Ophthalmology & Optometry, State Key Laboratory of Ophthalmology, Optometry and Visual Science, Wenzhou Medical University, 270 Xueyuan Xi Road, Wenzhou, Zhejiang 325027, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Hangzhou Zheda Femtosecond Test Co. Ltd., Zhejiang University National Science Park, Hangzhou, Zhejiang 310013, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Hangzhou Zheda Femtosecond Test Co. Ltd., Zhejiang University National Science Park

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Hangzhou Zheda Femtosecond Test Co. Ltd., Zhejiang University National Science Park, Hangzhou, Zhejiang 310013, China.

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: L. Qian, Z. Zhen, and S. Tang contributed equally.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The design of a fluorescent system for tracking valine remains a challenge till date. This work demonstrated the first use of the metal complex of dopamine-functionalized nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs-DA) as coordination-driven fluorescent platform for selective detection of valine in human serum and real food samples. The fluorescence of the N-GQDs-DA was quenched after the addition of Cr3+, which acts as both a fluorescence quencher and a receptor for target molecule. The quenched fluorescence of the N-GQDs-DA/Cr3+ sensing system was linearly restored as the valine concentration increased from 1.33 × 10−4 M to 62.5 × 10−4 M. Different coordination states of Cr3+ opened or blocked a PET course, which constructed “turn-off or turn-on” fluorescence switching. The “INHIBIT” logic gate operation can be carried out using the Cr3+ and valine as inputs and the emission bands EM 315 nm and EM 440 nm as outputs, indicating that they have promising applications in intelligent dual analyte sensing systems. MALDI-TOF mass spectra elucidated the formation of a low-fluorescent species [C55H32Cl3Cr2N4O10]+ and a high-fluorescent species [C70H58Cr2N7O14]+ without and with valine, respectively. This methodology provides a new concept for the design and development of highly selective optical system for single amino acid.

ce:section.ce:para: Valine is naturally occurring and essential amino acids that is vital for vertebrates. It is a transcription factor that regulates protein synthesis in porcine mammary epithelial cells [1]. The binding of valine to Rel protein affects the survival of mycobacterium tuberculosis related the tuberculosis disease [2]. Valine catabolism is essential for cancer cells [3]. Additionally, the diary of valine is associated with heart disease [4]. It has been reported that the Alzheimer type patients had the level of valine below 13 ± 3 mg/L [5]. Therefore, it is critical to monitor the amount of valine in biological systems and foods. Although the valine can be detected by a chromatographical method (i.e., HPLC-MS/MS), it requires complicated treatment to form valine derivatives [6]. Such a method cannot be used to monitor valine in vivo. In comparison, the fluorescent method is more flexible, affordable, and effective. It is often used to track the location of biomolecules in cells and living organs.

A few literatures discussed the optical determination of valine. A Zn(II) complex of bisbinaphthyl has been employed in the measurement of valine in CH3CN solutions [7]. The dansyl fluorophore 6-deoxy-6-N-(Na-[(5-dimethylamino-1-naphthalenesulfonyl)aminoethyl]-aminocylamino-b-cyclodextrin can recognize copper valine complex [8]. However, the design of fluorescence probes for valine is not desirable as a theoretical direction [9]. Many sensors for amino acids suffer from low solubility/dispersion in water, interference by other amino acids, and low sensitivity [10]. The design of fluorescent sensors for single amino acid remains a great challenge.

In previous studies, we discovered that graphene-based nanomaterials were suitable for fabricating fluorescent sensors for amino acids [11]. Graphene oxide often quenched the fluorescence of a fluorophore and was used to fabricate an off–on sensor [12], whereas graphene quantum dots (GQDs) showed strong fluorescence suitable for on–off–on sensors [13]. It has been demonstrated that the high quantum yield of graphene-based nanomaterials can produce a strong fluorescence intensity, hence increasing the sensitivity of targeted analytes with a low limit of detection (LOD) and wide dynamic ranges [14–16]. In addition, doping GQDs with nitrogen atoms (N-GQDs) results in an increase in the number of electron-enriched active sites, which supports a high yield of radiative recombination [17]. Several studies have used the N-GQDs or their derivatives to detect transition metal ions via coordination or electrostatic attraction [18]. This is often the case when the fluorescence of N-GQDs is quenched by photoinduced electron transfer (PET) or the inner filter effect, resulting in an “on–off” state [19]. The “off” state can be switched to an “on” state by chelating organic compounds with metal ions, which suppresses fluorescence quenching. The N-GQDs have been reported to be used for “on–off–on” detection of Cr(VI) ion and ascorbic acid [20]. Picolinate/Cr3+ decreased the fluorescence of N-GQDs via PET by chelating Cr3+ to N-GQDs, and cholesterol suppressed the PET course, inducing recovery of the fluorescence. The characteristic kinetic inertness to substitution of the Cr3+ d2sp3 octahedrical complex prevented the interference of many biological molecules such as saccharides [21,22]. Also, trivalent chromium is considered as a fundamental trace element for normal physiological function. No alterations in various hematological indices were observed at a dose of 1 mg/kg per day [23].

The preceding reports enlightened us on how to design a valine sensor via on–off–on detection using Cr3+ as a quencher and an N-GQD derivative as a fluorophore. Herein, a novel fluorescence sensing system based on dopamine (DA) functionalized N-GQDs (N-GQDs-DA)/Cr3+ for the selective detection of valine was developed in this work. The modifying of DA reduced the amount of carboxylate in the N-GQDs and acted as Cr3+ ligand. The detection of valine with high selectivity and sensitivity is via a PET signaling mechanism. As schematically shown in Scheme 1, the N-GQDs-DA fluorephore acts as an electron donor in N-GQDs-DA/Cr3+, while the metal cations lose their outer electrons and left the unoccupied orbitals acting as electron acceptor. They construct a PET sensor. Once binding with the target molecules (valine), the electron transfer from the N-GQDs-DA to the metal ions (associated fluorescent quenching process) is disrupted, due to the “blocking” effect induced by coordination of the target molecule to the N-GQDs-DA/Cr3+ at the metal centers, leading to a significantly enhanced fluorescence emission. In such a course, the fluorescence intensity of the N-GQDs-DA sensing system decreased significantly as the Cr3+ concentration increased (off state). The quenched fluorescence of the N-GQDs-DA/Cr3+ sensing system was gradually recovered in the presence of valine (on state). Additionally, the selectivity of the sensing system for valine was assessed in the presence of interferences. Furthermore, the N-GQDs-DA/Cr3+ sensing system was not only successfully used to detect valine in human serum and living cells, but was also used to determine valine in foods. More importantly, this sensing system can be used as a two-input INHIBIT logic gate in the current system.

All chemical reagents are analytically pure and can be used as received without additional purification. The chemicals were acquired from Aladdin Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Millipore ultrapure water was used throughout the experiments. All solutions used in the experiments were freshly prepared. Diluted HCl and NaOH were used to alter the pHs of the solutions.

The transmission electron microscope (TEM) images of N-GQDs-DA were measured at a 150 kV accelerating voltage using a PHILIPS EM400ST instrument. The height of the prepared N-GQDs was determined by atomic force microscopy (AFM) (SPI3800N) in the tapping mode. Renishaw inViaQontor obtained the Raman spectra using a 532 nm laser device. The ESCALB MK-II spectrometer was used to characterize X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The Hitachi F-4600 fluorometer was used to obtain fluorescence emission spectra. The XRD spectrum was collected by a Bruker D2 PHASER powder diffractometer with a Cu Kα radiation source. UV–vis spectra were recorded through the Agilent Cary 100 spectrometer. MALDI-TOF mass spectra were measured by the Autoflex mass spectrometer using delayed extraction and a nitrogen laser.

The N-GQDs were prepared by the method previously reported [24]. After mixing EDC (1 mmol) and NHS (1 mmol) for 10 min at room temperature, the mixture was added to the N-GQDs solution (2 mg/mL) while vigorously stirring. The coupling reaction was carried out when DA (2 mmol) was added to aforementioned solution for 24 h at room temperature with vigorous stirring. The resulting solution was dialyzed in the 1 kDa dialysis bag for three days and stored at 4 °C in the refrigerator.

Various concentration of Cr3+ was added to the N-GQDs-DA solution (20 mg/L) at room temperature to detect Cr3+. The resulting solution was thoroughly mixed and then incubated at room temperature for 5 min. The change in fluorescence intensity was measured without and with Cr3+ at the emission peaks of 315 nm and 440 nm in the presence of 279 nm illumination.

A fluorescence sensing device was developed by incubating N-GQDs-DA solution with a certain concentration of Cr3+ for 5 min. This resulted in the formation of the N-GQDs-DA/Cr3+ sensing device. Then, valine was added in different concentrations to the N-GQDs-DA/Cr3+ sensing system and thoroughly mixed. Following that, the final mixture was incubated at room temperature for 5 min. The fluorescence spectra were measured at 279 nm for excitation and 315 nm and 440 nm for emission, respectively. The following equation was used to calculate LOD.
LOD = 3σ/ slope (1).
where σ is the standard deviation.

Cell cytotoxic results of N-GQDs-DA incubated with human retinal pigment epithelium (ARPE-19) cells were evaluated using cell counting Kit-8 (CCK-8 assay) with different concentrations and times as described in the previous work [25].

ARPE-19 cells were planted in 96 well plates for 24 h at 37 °C and 5 % CO2. The cells were incubated with the N-GQDs-DA solution for 2 h and rinsed three times with phosphate buffered saline, and then the cells were treated with different concentrations of Cr3+ for another 30 min. Following that, confocal laser scanning microscopy was used to image the cells. To detect valine in living cells, cells treated with N-GQDs-DA/Cr3+ solution were rinsed three times and then incubated for another 30 min with different concentrations of valine. Confocal laser scanning microscopy was used to obtain the cell images.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of valine in the real samples
ce:section.ce:para: To examine the practical feasibility of using the N-GQDs-DA/Cr3+ sensing system to detect valine in real samples, tap water, milk powder, and honey were selected and collected from a nearby location (Wenzhou, China). The tap water was filtered using 0.22 µm filtered-membranes prior to use. The food sample was pretreated as previously reported [26]. The standard Cr3+ or valine solution was mixed with real samples and then incubated for 5 min at room temperature with N-GQDs-DA or N-GQDs-DA/Cr3+ solution. The results were measured three times in parallel.

The morphology images of N-GQDs-DA were characterized by TEM and AFM. The TEM image shows that the as-prepared N-GQDs-DA is uniformly distributed with an even particle size of 3 nm ( Fig. 1a). The AFM result shows that the N-GQDs-DA has a height of approximately 2.0 nm, which corresponds to 5–6 graphene stacked layers (Fig. 1b). Raman data analysis shows that the D band and G band at 1362 cm−1 and 1588 cm−1, respectively, correspond to the presence of sp3 defect at the graphene edge and sp2 hybridized carbon atoms in the graphene plane (Fig. 1c). The ID/IG ratio of 1.05 for N-GQDs-DA is higher than 0.89 for N-GQDs [24], indicating that structural distortion of the graphene sheets has occurred due to the incorporation of DA. The XRD result shows a characteristic diffraction peak at 23.4° corresponding to the (002) plane (Fig. 1d), with neighboring layers separated by 0.37 nm.

The measured XPS survey spectra of both N-GQDs and N-GQDs-DA clearly showed three peaks at approximately 283 eV, 398 eV, and 530 eV, corresponding to carbon (C 1 s), nitrogen (N 1 s), and oxygen (O 1 s) (Fig. S1). The N-GQDs contained carbon, nitrogen, and oxygen content of 79.3 %, 6.07 %, and 11.87 %, respectively. The high-resolution C1s peaks of N-GQDs-DA corresponding to CC/C-C, C-OH, C-N, CO, and N-CO were observed at 284.6 eV, 285.95 eV, 286.45 eV, 287.6 eV, and 288.2 eV, respectively. The N1s high-resolution peaks of N-GQDs-DA at 399.75 eV, 401.50 eV, and 402.20 eV were assigned to pyrrolic N, graphitic N, and C-N, respectively. In comparison to N-GQDs, the carbon content of N-GQDs-DA decreased to 78.1 %, but the nitrogen and oxygen levels increased to 6.86 % and 12.01 %, respectively. It was shown that DA chemically bonded effectively with the N-GQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing behavior of N-GQDs-DA/Cr3+ to valine and molecular mechanism
ce:section.ce:para: The UV–vis spectra of N-GQDs, DA, and N-GQDs-DA are shown in Fig. 2a. The N-GQDs showed an n-π * transition of CO (related to heteroatom p-π conjugation) at 326 nm [27], while the DA showed a shoulder peak at 218 nm and a typical peak at 279 nm, respectively, corresponding to the π-π * transition of CC and the n-π * transition of phenol groups [28]. The adsorption peaks of N-GQDs-DA showed the characteristics of both N-GQDs and DA. The peak at 279 nm related to DA also appeared in N-GQDs-DA. However, the peak at 326 nm was displaced to 334 nm for N-GQDs-DA, indicating the formation of an amide (-CO-NH-) in the conjugation system [29].

When excited at 279 nm, N-GQDs-DA showed two emission peaks at 315 nm (EM 315 nm) and 440 nm (EM 440 nm). The peak of 315 nm corresponded to the emission of DA [30], and the peak at 440 nm corresponded to the arising from the π*-n transition of the N-GQDs moiety [31]. When varying concentrations of Cr3+ were added at pH of 6 ( Fig. 3a), the fluorescence intensities of the two peaks decreased as the Cr3+ concentration increased. The linear relationship between relative fluorescence intensity at EM 315 nm and EM 440 nm and Cr3+ concentrations was formed (insert pictures in Fig. 3a). The linear regression equation (F-F0)/F0 = 0.01849 × CCr3+ + 0.22 (at EM 315 nm) and (F − F0)/F0 = 0.02043 × CCr3+ + 0.393 (at EM 440 nm) were obtained with a linear range of 1.66 × 10−4 M to 23.8 × 10−4 M. Correlation coefficients were 0.998 and 0.989, respectively. The detection limit of Cr3+ was 4.56 μM. The UV–vis peak at 279 nm of N-GQDs-DA increased significantly with the addition of Cr3+ (Fig. S2a). However, the peak at 334 nm decreased. Two new peaks at 413 and 577 nm appeared assigning to the LMCT and d-d transitions of Cr3+. Compared to those peaks of aqueous CrCl3 at 429 and 607 nm (Fig. 2b), a blue shift occurred in N-GQDs-DA/Cr3+ complex. It has been well-known that the H2O and Cl− are weak field ligands, whereas the phenolic hydroxyl and amine are strong field ligands [32]. The replacement of weak field ligands by strong field ligands induces a blue shift in the spectra of LMCT and d-d transitions of Cr3+ [33]. These findings indicated that the phenolic groups on the DA moiety of N-GQDs-DA were deprotonated, chelating to Cr3+ [34]. And the amide participated in coordination. The fluorescence lifetime of the N-GQDs-DA decreased from 7.584 ns to 4.699 ns (Fig. S3) in the absence and presence of Cr3+, indicating an energy transfer from the donor N-GQDs-DA to the acceptor Cr3+ ions, which initiated a PET course.

The fluorescence change of N-GQDs-DA was also examined at different temperature after the addition of various concentrations of Cr3+. The quenching mechanism was confirmed by the Stern–Volmer equation (Fig. S4), F0/F = 1 + Ksv[Q]. Where “F0” denotes the florescence intensity of the N-GQDs-DA in the absence of Cr3+ and “F” denotes the florescence intensity of the N-GQDs-DA in the presence of Cr3+, respectively. Ksv is the Stern–Volmer quenching constant and [Q] is the concentration of Cr3+. The slope of the plot represents the Stern-Volmer constant, which is calculated to be 666 M−1 at 298.15 K, 320 M−1 at 308.15 K, and 196 M−1 at 318.15 K. Ksv was calculated to be 2106 M−1, 1214 M−1, and 966 M−1 at 298.15 K, 308.15 K, and 318.15 K, respectively, for 440 nm. The regression correlation coefficients are all higher than 0.99. The calculated Ksv constants decrease as the temperature increases from 298.15 K to 318.15 K, showing that the quenching mechanism is static [35]. These observations proved the formation of a Cr3+ complex with N-GQDs-DA. With decreasing pH values, H+ prevented the Cr3+ from coordination with N-GQDs-DA, resulting in a decrease in fluorescence changes (Fig. S5). At pH values of 6–7, the fluorescence intensity changes were similar. Higher pH values were not determined to avoid the hydrolysis of Cr3+ ions. As a result, a pH of 6 was selected for sensing valine in aqueous solutions.

Further addition of valine to the solution of N-GQDs-DA/Cr3+ increased the fluorescence at the peaks of 315 nm and 440 nm. Different amount of N-GQDs-DA (10 mg/L, 20 mg/L, and 30 mg/L) showed similar behaviors (Fig. S6). But high concentration of N-GQDs-DA easily aggregated in cation solutions and 20 mg/L is adapted. It is worth noting that an excess of Cr3+ can have a detrimental effect on the detection of valine due to coordination. As a result, the optimal Cr3+ concentration was determined. When the concentrations of N-GQDs-DA and valine were fixed (Fig. S7), the fluorescence enhancement efficiency of N-GQDs-DA/Cr3+ was found to be proportional to the initial Cr3+ concentration in the range of 0.1–2.1 mM. The fluorescence enhancement efficiency of N-GQDs-DA/Cr3+ by addition of valine increased gradually as Cr3+ concentration was increased from 0.1 to 1.6 mM. However, the fluorescence began to decrease after the Cr3+ concentration was increased to 1.6 mM. Therefore, 1.6 mM was used as the optimal concentration for valine detection in the sensing system. The quenched fluorescence of the N-GQDs-DA/Cr3+ sensing system was gradually recovered by adding various concentrations of valine between 0 and 62.5 × 10−4 M (Fig. 3b). The linear regression equation for the valine detection was developed throughout the concentration range of 6.62–62.5 × 10−4 M at EM 315 nm and 1.33–16.4 × 10−4 M at EM 440 nm, respectively (the insert pictures in Fig. 3b). The linear regression equation was calculated to be (F − F0)/F0 = 0.01082 × Cvaline + 0.8570 (at EM 315 nm) and (F − F0)/F0 = 0.03518 × Cvaline + 0.4223 (at EM 440 nm) with regression coefficient of 0.999 and 0.979, respectively. The LOD for valine was 13.6 μM. Interestingly, the intensity at 315 nm increased significantly more than the intensity at 440 nm. Although the free DA can bind the Cr3+, it did not show any noticeable changes in the fluorescent emission intensity of 315 nm upon addition of the Cr3+ and valine in sequence (Fig. S8). Additionally, the fluorescence lifetime of the N-GQDs-DA/Cr3+ sensing system increased from 4.699 ns to 6.392 ns in the presence of valine (Fig. S3). This could be explained by the coordination of valine to Cr3+. Consequently, the fluorescence intensity at 315 nm was restored by suppressing PET (restraining electron transfer from the phenolic hydroxyl of DA on N-GQDs-DA to Cr3+ ions). The UV–vis spectra of N-GQDs-DA/Cr3+ at 279 nm decreased by titration of valine, corroborating the above observations (Fig. S2b).

The coordination of Cr3+ to N-GQDs-DA was further validated, as was the binding of valine, using a matrix assisted laser desorption ionization time of flight mass spectrometer (MALDI-TOF MS). N-GQDs-DA was detected as an ion at m/z 839.8 ([N-GQDs-DA + H]+), as shown in Fig. 4. After dopamine was removed and a hydroxyl group was added, the N-GQDs was found at m/z 703.7 ([N-GQDs + H]+). The addition of CrCl3 resulted in a m/z measurement of 1119.8, consistent with the species [(N-GQDs-DA lost 2 H) + Cr2Cl3(H2O)4]+. Due to the fact that the experiments were carried out at pH 6 to avoid the precipitation of Cr3+, very low amount of free OH− was present. As a result, the dopamine hydroxyl groups tended to deprotonate to coordinate the Cr3+ resulting in a decrease in fluorescence at 315 nm. The deprotonation of hydroxyl groups in dopamine neutralized the charge of metal ions. The decomposition of [(N-GQDs-DA lost 2 H) + Cr2Cl3(H2O)4]+ yielded m/z values of 1083.8 and 1071.5, corresponding to [(N-GQDs-DA lost 2 H) + Cr2Cl3(H2O)2]+ and [(N-GQDs-DA lost 2H1N) + Cr2Cl3(H2O)2]+. Previous studies indicated that the ortho phenol groups of dopamine chelated the transition metals were stable because of the formation of a five atoms ring [36]. Coordination of transition metals with DA accompanied by deprotonation of phenol groups. The addition of valinate produced a high m/z due to coordination (Fig. 4c). Three valinates ions replaced the three chloride ions, yielding the ion [(N-GQDs-DA lost 2 H) + Cr2(val)3(H2O)2]+ with a m/z of 1325.7. Two valinates replacement indicated the presence of the species [(N-GQDs-DA lost 2 H) + Cr2(val)2(H2O)Cl]+ with a m/z of 1225.8. These observations corroborated the metal chelating mechanism hypothesized from fluorescence and UV–vis analysis. More explanation of detected m/z and calculated Mw of various species were listed in Table S1. On the basis of the foregoing, a proposed molecular structure for N-GQDs-DA was shown in Fig. 4d.

To further examine the selectivity of N-GQDs-DA/Cr3+ sensing system for the detection of valine, competitive experiments in the presence of various interferents were carried out (Fig. S9). The fluorescence recovery of the N-GQDs-DA/Cr3+ sensing system was tested by adding possible interferent natural amino acids to the aqueous solution. The N-GQDs-DA/Cr3+ sensing system showed high selectivity for valine detection. Although the presence of 5 equivalent mole ratio of lysine increases the luminescent intensity about 5 %, the influence was insignificant. The relative fluorescence intensity of the N-GQDs-DA/Cr3+ sensing system was unaffected by the addition of valine in the presence of 5 equivalent mole ratio of the other amino acids. These competitive experiments showed that coexistence has no interference on the fluorescence recovery response for detection of valine, indicating that the N-GQDs-DA/Cr3+ sensing system has a higher selectivity for sensing valine than for sensing the other amino acids. So far, the solution chemistry of different amino acids chelating to Cr3+ cannot be ruled out due to diverse species of amino acids and complicated ligand exchange course [37,38]. But our measurements exhibited that the valine produced a dramatical change in the fluorescent emission intensity of N-GQDs-DA/Cr3+ via coordination. It has been reported that reaction between valine and hexaaqueochromium(III) had lower enthalpy and higher entropy than the other amino acids examined. Such properties favoured ligand exchange rather than water exchange of Cr3+ complex [39,40]. Additionally, a side-by-side comparison of some biological trace transition metals were investigated for their effects on the fluorescence emission of N-GQDs-DA. Fig. S10 indicated that the Cu2+, Fe3+ and Fe2+ also quenched the fluorescence of N-GQDs-DA similar to Cr3+. The quenching efficiency sequence is Fe3+ > Cu2+ > Cr3+ > Fe2+. But the Zn2+ and Mn2+ had insignificant effects on the fluorescence change of N-GQDs-DA. The fact of fluorescence quenching of N-GQDs-DA by Fe3+ was also observed in a previous report on Fe3+ quenching the fluorescence of GODs-DA [41]. However, the Fe3+ quenching the emission peak at 460 nm of GQDs-DA differed from our case at 315 nm (associated with dihydroxyphenyl moiety) and at 440 nm. Addition of valine into the individual solutions of N-GQDs-DA/Cu2+, N-GQDs-DA/Fe3+, N-GQDs-DA/Fe2+, N-GQDs-DA/Zn2+ and N-GQDs-DA/Mn2+, the fluorescence changes of respective N-GQDs-DA/metal were − 15 %, + 64 %, + 20 %, − 5 %, and − 14 %. In the same condition, the fluorescence change of N-GQDs-DA/Cr3+ with valine was 157 %. These phenomena suggested that Cu2+, Zn2+, Fe2+, and Mn2+ did not affect the application of N-GQDs-DA/Cr3+ to detect valine. Interestingly, the addition of valine into the N-GQDs-DA/Fe3+ induced the fluorescence peak shifting to 307 nm, and the intensity increasing about 64 %. Linear relationship between the amount of Fe3+ and N-GQDs-DA at 315 nm was not found. It is well-known that the Fe3+ tends to form Fe(OH)3 at a pH above 1.9 [42]. The effect of Fe3+ on the fluorescence of N-GQDs-DA might differ from the other cations used. And the blood seldom has Fe3+. The influence of Fe3+ is minor in biological applications.

The developed fluorescence quenching and recovery processes can be performed to detect the Cr3+ and valine in the solution with turn off/on switch. An “INHIBIT” logic gate including of OR and NOT logical functions was constructed successfully based on the above experiment results. As shown in Fig. S11, two inputs including Cr3+ (input 1) and valine (input 2) were defined. The presence of Cr3+ and valine are assumed as “1” and the absence are assumed as “0.” The two outputs were fluorescence emission at 315 nm and 440 nm. Outputs are set as “1” (on state) and “0” (off state) when the emission intensity is above and below the threshold value 100, respectively. When any input is absent (0, 0), both outputs are activated and are denoted as (1, 1). Upon addition of Cr3+ into the solution(1, 0), the fluorescence intensity at 315 nm and 440 nm obviously decreased and was lower than the threshold value 100. Thus, no output was found and denoted by (0, 0). Furthermore, the fluorescence intensity at 315 nm and 440 nm remains higher than the threshold in the presence of valine input and both outputs were denoted by (1, 1). In the presence of Cr3+ and valine input (1, 1), the fluorescence intensity at 315 nm is higher than the threshold value and fluorescence intensity at 440 nm is lower than the threshold value, which generated the output state (1, 0). The corresponding truth table is illustrated in Fig. S9c.

The blood concentration of valine is about 1.2 mM [43], which is in the dynamic linear concentration response range of the N-GQDs-DA/Cr3+ for valine. To explore the biological applications of fluorescence sensing system, N-GQDs-DA/Cr3+ was investigated to detect valine in human serum samples. Although the blood concentration of Cr3+ (about 0.01 mM) [44] is out of the dynamic linear concentration response range, it still needs to study the behaviors of Cr3+ in the presence of N-GQDs-DA in serum. The N-GQDs-DA was mixed with human serum at a concentration of 1.0 g/L. The fluorescence intensity of N-GQDs-DA sensing system significantly decreased with increasing Cr3+ concentration as shown in the Fig. S12. After addition of valine, the fluorescence intensity of N-GQDs-DA/Cr3+ sensing system gradually recovered in serum. The linear regression equation was (F − F0)/F0 = 0.02588 × Cval + 0.1518 and (F − F0)/F0 = 0.00516 × Cval + 0.22874 at EM 315 nm and EM 440 nm, respectively. The correlation coefficient was all 0.998. The linear regression equation for the valine detection was in the concentration range of 4.75–79.5 × 10−4 M at EM 315 nm and 5.10–79.5 × 10−4 M at EM 440 nm, respectively. These results indicated that N-GQDs-DA/Cr3+ was able to detect valine in human serum. The recovery ratios for valine were exhibited in the range of 96.3–104.0 % in the Table S2.

To evaluate the feasibility of fluorescence sensor in living cell imaging, the cell cytotoxicity studies of N-GQDs-DA was carried out using the CCK8 assays. As can be seen from the Fig. S13, cell cytotoxicity was examined with different incubation time and different concentrations. The cell viability is found above 80 % even at a concentration of 100 μg/mL for 72 h of incubation time. These results confirmed that N-GQDs-DA are good biocompatible and low toxic to the ARPE-19 cells. The concentration of 1.6 mM was also acceptable in biological studies [45]. The observations indicated good biocompatibility and low toxicity of the N-GQDs-DA and suitable for detecting valine in living cells.

For intracellular detection of valine, the cells treated with N-GQDs-DA/Cr3+ solution acted as control group, as shown in Fig. 5. It can be found that the cells of control group showed weak fluorescence signal. After exogenous valine was added to the cells incubated with N-GQDs-DA/Cr3+ solution, the evident fluorescence signal was observed. Furthermore, an obvious fluorescence enhancement was found when the cells were treated with different concentrations of valine. The confocal microscopy characterization results reveals that no obvious cells morphology change was found. The experiments results suggested that N-GQDs-DA/Cr3+ sensing system was good biocompatible and can be applied to detect intracellular valine in living cells.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection in tap water, milk, and honey
ce:section.ce:para: The Cr3+ and valine are commonly used as nutrition in food. The practical applicability and reliability of sensing system were verified by detecting concentrations of Cr3+ and valine in real samples. Cr3+ and valine were detected in real samples (tap water, milk, and honey) at concentration ranging from 1 to 5 mM. The recovery ratios for Cr3+ and valine were exhibited in the range of 96–104.2 % in the Table S3. The standard deviation of the relative standard deviation ranged from 1.08 % to 4.32 %. These results indicate that sensing system is both reliable and practicable, and that it can be used as an alternative method for detecting Cr3+ and valine in real samples without evident interference.

In summary, we developed a coordination-driven fluorescent system for detecting valine in aqueous solution. It was notable that the addition of Cr3+ could obviously quench the fluorescence of the N-GQDs-DA sensing system, and that the fluorescence can be restored after adding valine into the N-GQDs-DA/Cr3+ system. The sensing system showed high selectivity and sensitivity for detecting valine rather than other amino acids. The Cr3+ chelating to N-GQDs-DA opened a PET course, while the coordination of valine to N-GQDs-DA/Cr3+ blocked it resulting in fluorescence recovery. It can also be constructed as an “INHIBIT” logic gate, using EM 315 nm and EM 440 nm as outputs and Cr3+ and valine as inputs. Additionally, the sensing system was able to detect valine in human serum, living cells and real samples (such as tap water, milk, and honey). These results showed that sensing system has a promising future in real-time monitoring of real food samples for ensuing food safety and tracking Cr3+ and valine in vivo. This work represents a breakthrough in the design of on–off–on system for detecting single amino acids in human serums, cells and foods.

Liangliang Qian: Performed the experiments, analyzed data, wrote the paper. Zhengan Zhen: performed the experiments, analyzed data, wrote the paper. Shipeng Tang: Performed the experiments, validation, revised the paper. Chaoqun Zhou: Performed the titration experiments. Man Ji: cell imagine and cytotoxicity study. Bin Liu: Validation, Formal analysis, logic gate. Yuying Fang: Validation, Formal analysis. Shengju Ou: Methodology, Formal analysis, revised the manuscript and editing. Rumei Cheng: Conceptualization, Methodology, Formal analysis, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition. All authors have approved the final version of the manuscript. The corresponding author is responsible for ensuring that the descriptions are accurate and agreed by all authors.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare no competing financial interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The National Natural Science Foundation of China (21405115), the Wenzhou Bureau of Science and Technology (Y20210081), the Technology Project of Zhejiang Province Medical and Health Project (2015KYB254, 2017KY492), Project of State Key Laboratory of Ophthalmology, Optometry and Vision Science of Wenzhou Medical University (J02-20210202) and Ophthalmology & Optometry of Wenzhou Medical University (YNCX201408) are acknowledged.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.snb.2022.132168.

ce:section.ce:para:

.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Liangliang Qian is a graduate student at the School of Optometry of Wenzhou Medical University. His current research involves nano biosensors and logic gate construction.
Zhengan Zhen is a graduate student at Wenzhou Medical University. He investigated the fluorescent mechanism of carbon-based nanosensors.

Shipeng Tang is a graduate student majoring in biomedical engineering at Wenzhou Medical University. His current research topics include fabrication of amino functionalized graphene and molecular logic gates.

Chaoqun Zhou studied material science at the South China University of Technology and received her master’s degree. In 2020, she became a PhD candidate in the Laboratory for Nanotechnology Applications at Wenzhou Medical University. Her current research topics include construction and logic gate operation of fluorescence detection system for biological small molecule recognition using graphene doped quantum dots.

Man Ji is a graduate student majoring in ophthalmology at Wenzhou Medical University. Her current research topic include graphite carbon nitride, glucose mimetic enzyme, photodynamics therapy and tumor.

Liu Bin studied computer science and technology in Jiangxi Agricultural University. In his bachelor's thesis, he studied the development of online fruit mall system. He is now a graduate student in the School of Ophthalmology of Wenzhou Medical University.

Yuying Fang studied pharmaceutical engineering at Anhui University of Science and Technology. In 2021, she started her master at the Laboratory for Nanotechnology Applications at Wenzhou Medical University. Her current research involves the development of novel amino acid fluorescent agent and in vivo imaging application.

Prof. Dr. Shengju Ou completed his Ph.D. in inorganic chemistry in 2006 from the Nanjing University. Thereafter, he joined the Guangxi Teacher’s University and studied the biosensors for amino acids and saccharides. Subsequently, he joined the Zhejiang University National Science Park, where he established Femtosecond Research Center. His current research interest is molecular recognition mechanisms.

Prof. Dr. Rumei Cheng studied chemistry at Tongji University and received her Ph.D. She has been working at the School of Optometry of Wenzhou Medical University since 2010. She focuses on design and fabrication of nanostructural optical sensors for disease diagnostics. From 2019–2020, she was involved in lab-on-a-chip-research and the development of molecular diagnostic techniques at University of New South Wales, Sydney. In 2020, she became a group leader of Carbon-based Optical Nanodevices for Sensors at the School of Optometry of Wenzhou Medical University.
